q: Carbohydrates, proteins, and nucleic acids are synthesis polymers.
Plastic, fibers, and elastomers are synthesis polymers.
The characteristic odour of cherries is due to benzaldehyde.
An alcohol is an organic compound with an O atom bonded to two alkyl groups.
Ethers have the general formula CnH2n+2O2.
Ethers and alcohols are the same general formula.
In the IUPAC system, the oxygen atom and the larger alkyl group are named as an alkoxy substituent.
Ethers can be obtained by dehydration of alcohols.
Ether molecules have intermolecular hydrogen bonding between molecules.
The boiling points of ethers much lower than alcohol of similar molecular mass.
All ethers are soluble in water.
Ethers are more polar than the alcohols with the same molecular mass.
Alcohols and ethers contain oxygen with only single bonds.
Ethers are more soluble in water than alcohols.
The lower homogenous series of ethers are highly volatile and flammable.
Ethers are not organic solvents.
Simple ether, diethyl ether is odourless.
Aldehydes and ketones both contain the carbonyl group located at the end of a carbon chain.
In ketones, a carbonyl group can be located within a carbon chain rather than at the end.
Aldehydes and ketones have the general formula CnH2nO.
Organic compounds ending with -ol describe compounds with aldehyde functional groups.
The simplest ketone is acetone.
The IUPAC name of acetaldehyde is methanal.
The IUPAC name of acetone is propanone.
The oxidation of secondary alcohol gives aldehydes.
The ketone product is not needed to be distilled of immediately.
At room temperature, methanal is a gas and ethanal is a volatile liquid.
A nitrile contains a carbon-nitrogen single bond.
Nitriles are the organic compounds also known as amino compounds.
Inorganic compounds with -CN group are called cyanides.
Formalin is the 40 % aqueous solution of ethanal.
Ketones are not organic solvent.
Fehling's solution is used to distinguish between ketones and amines.
Carboxylic acids are organic compounds with a carbonyl functional group.
The carboxyl group is made up of a carbonyl group and a hydroxyl group.
The general formula of carboxylic acid is CnH2n+2O2.
The IUPAC name of acetic acid is ethanoic acid.
Carboxylic acids are made from both primary and secondary alcohols.
Carboxylic acids with four or fewer carbons are soluble in water.
Carboxylic acids are weak acids.
Carboxylic acids have weak intermolecular hydrogen bonds between molecules.
Carboxylic acids have lower boiling points than alcohols with the similar molecular mass.
Ethanoic acid is stronger acid than ethanol.
Carboxylic acids react with carbonates to give salt and water.
Esters are organic compounds with a carboxyl functional group.
The general formula of esters is R-COOR'.
Esters can be derived from the carboxylic acid and the alcohol.
Esters are very soluble in water.
The solubility of esters in water increases rapidly with an increase in molecular mass.
Sulphuric acid acts as a catalyst for the esterification of alcohol and carboxylic acid.
The boiling points of esters are higher than those of the corresponding parent acids.
Esterification is simply the reverse of acidic hydrolysis of esters.
An ester is hydrolysed by using an aqueous acid to yield a carboxylic acid and a water.
Basic hydrolysis of esters is a reversible reaction.
Hydrolysis of ester leads to the formation of alcohol and carboxylic acid in an alkaline condition.
The esterification is the reaction of the ester with an alcohol in the presence of an acid catalyst.
Ester has a characteristic odour of fruits.
Esters mixed with NaOH produce soap.
The simplest amine is ethylamine.
Amines are characterized by the presence of the -OH group.
The IUPAC name of iso-propylamine is 1-propanamine.
The smell of liquid amines possesses a fishy smell.
Primary and secondary amines can form intermolecular hydrogen bonds, but tertiary amines cannot.
Primary amines are more soluble in water than secondary and tertiary amines.
Primary amines have lower boiling points than those of ethers of a similar molecular mass.
The boiling points of secondary amines are lower than those of alcohols which have the same number of carbon atoms.
Alcohol molecules have hydrogen atoms bonded to a more electronegative nitrogen atom.
The amide is refluxed with hydrochloric acid to form a carboxylic and an amine.
The amides form strong ionic bond and have high boiling points and melting points.
The water solubility of amides increases with increasing carbon chain length.
All simple amides are solids except methanamide.
Aqueous solutions of amides are basic properties.
Amides can be hydrolysed in acidic solutions only.
Hydrocarbons include aliphatic hydrocarbons and aromatic hydrocarbons.
The general formula of an ester is CnH2nO2 which the same a carboxylic acid with same carbon number.
The presence of a carbonyl group can be easily tested by using Fehling's solution.
Fehling's solution can be used to distinguish aldehyde and ketone functional groups.
IR spectroscopy can identify functional groups quick and easily.
Infrared spectroscopy can be used to determine the size and shape of a compound's carbon skeleton.
When the frequency of infrared light matches the frequency of bond vibration in a molecule, a peak appears on the spectrum.
Alcohol functional groups has the highest frequency in an infrared (IR) spectrum.
A carbonyl group usually has an absorption in the range of 3200-3600 cm⁻¹.
High frequencies, large wavenumbers, and short wavelengths are associated with high energies.
The homonuclear diatomic molecules are IR active.
The heteronuclear diatomic molecules which have dipole moments are IR active.
A characteristic frequency depends on the bond strength and the masses of the atoms.
Light atoms vibrate at lower frequencies than heavier atoms.
The multiple bonds vibrate at lower frequencies than single bonds.
The stretching vibration vibrate at higher frequencies than bending vibration.
In IR spectrum, N-H and O-H stretches show up in the same wavenumber region.
In IR spectrum, O-H stretch is narrow and weak while the N-H stretch is broad and strong.
The bond of N-H is more polarity and stronger hydrogen than bond of O-H.
When the frequency of infrared radiation matches the frequency of bond vibration in a molecule, a peak appears on the spectrum.
Infrared spectroscopy can be used to determine the size and shape of a compound's carbon skeleton.
Infrared spectroscopy are destructive techniques compared to methods using chemical reactions.
Polymers are also called macromolecules that are made of many repeating units.
Synthetic fibre is polymer, while plastic is not a polymer.
If a polymer consists of only one kind of monomers, then it is called a homopolymer.
Unsaturated molecules can form addition polymers.
Polyethene is produced by the addition polymerisation of ethane monomers.
Teflon is an example of condensation polymers.
Terylene is an example of condensation polymers.
Terylene contains many ester linkages.
Nylon is an example of polyesters.
Nylon 6,6 is formed from the reaction of adipic acid and 1,6-diaminohexane.
Nylon is a polyamide as it contains many amide linkages.
A large protein molecule is one of the types of polyesters.
Starch is made from thousands of amino acid monomers.
There are 21 different amino acids commonly found in proteins.
The covalent bond formed between the two amino acids is called a peptide bond.
An amino acid unit within a polypeptide chain is called an amino acid residue.
Condensation polymers have higher molecular masses than addition polymers.
Terylene does not resistant to stretching and shrinking.
Plastics are particular types of polymers that can be moulded.
All plastics are relatively cheap, easily moulded into various shapes.
Plastics that are thrown into the sea endanger marine animals.
Plastics are readily decomposed.
Recycling symbols consist of three arrows around one of six numbers.
Recycling symbols are sometimes mentioned on plastic products.
Biodegradable plastics are made from monomers derived directly from plant materials.
Polylactic acid (PLA) is biodegradable plastic.
Biodegradable plastics contains carbonyl groups (C=O).
Polylactic acid (PLA) is made from fermented plant starch.
Polylactic acid (PLA) is petroleum-based plastics.
Polyethylene terephthalate is a photodegradable plastic.
